---
title: "Nome Placer Fields: Bear Cub (pilot)"
subtitle: "Recovering 1925-1955 paper drill-log data into a structured, geospatial dataset"
author: "Sky King"
date: "2026-04"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    number-sections: true
execute:
  warning: false
---

## Summary

Bear Cub is a small placer-gold claim in the Cape Nome mining district of Alaska. Between 1919 and 1955, three operators drilled twenty-four exploration holes on it; the paper drill logs sit in a family archive. This notebook turns those twenty-four sheets into a structured dataset — coordinates, depth-by-depth gold yields, bedrock contacts — and reproduces the resource estimates two prior analysts computed by hand on the broader property.

The key technical results:

- **Pay-zone-only resource estimate of 6,251 fine ounces (90% MC CI 6,010–6,739).** That's 38% below the published 10,056-ounce figure for the same property. The gap is *methodological*, not measurement-level — see [§Cross-validation](#cross-validation-shared-holes) and the [mining-engineer review notebook](mining_review.html). On the three holes where per-hole grades can be compared against two prior independent published analyses (2021 and 2024), agreement is within 6–10% when the same pay-zone window is assumed; the property-wide gap comes from how each calculation defines the pay layer.
- **Cross-checks catch OCR errors and a 45° geomorphology trap.** The pipeline projects each transcribed hole onto a 1936 cartographer's property map. Five OCR errors the model self-rated as "high confidence" turned up only because the holes plotted in the wrong place — wrong by 800-2,000 ft. A separate corner-label trap (the dh-map's image-axis labels don't match the patent's compass corners — they're rotated by one step) was caught the same way and required a re-derivation of the local-feet → WGS84 affine; corner round-trip residuals dropped from ~25 m to ~0.3 m once the labels were corrected.
- **The corpus reveals constants the operators never wrote down.** A single value — `282.6` — appears in 16 of the 24 logs, in the operator's hand-written conversion of milligrams to cents per cubic yard. It bundles the 1920s gold price, the drill bit's cross-section, and unit conversions into a single creek factor. Empirical derivation from the corpus, not stated explicitly on any individual log.
- **Cost.** OCR via Claude Opus 4.7 on every page: about $14 across all 24 logs, roughly $0.50-$1.00 per multi-page log. Manageable for an archive of this size; a 10,000-log archive would benefit from a hybrid pipeline (purpose-built OCR for raw text, an LLM pass for form-specific schema mapping) at roughly an order of magnitude lower cost.

The drill-log archive is family-held. The methodology and schema-only collar table are public; raw scans and per-interval gold yields are not. See [SOURCE.md](../../data/raw/bear_cub/SOURCE.md) for provenance.

For the full review notebook with TODOs, data caveats, and improvement notes: [`internal.qmd`](internal.qmd).

## Why this archive is interesting

A long-running observation in mineral exploration is that historical drilling data — paper logs from campaigns decades or even a century ago — rarely makes it into modern targeting models. Most of it sits in basements, government archives, and private files. The industry term for this material is *dark data*: structured information that exists on paper but isn't in any database. The current generation of AI-driven exploration teams treat dark-data ingestion as a competitive advantage, because the company that turns paper into structured data first gets to feed it into its targeting models first.

Bear Cub is a worked example of that step on a small, family-scale archive — small enough to hand-validate end-to-end against two independent prior analyses.

## The archive

Twenty-four paper drill logs from the Murray group, on the Bear Cub claim (BLM patent MS 1178), Cape Nome mining district on the southern Seward Peninsula. Drilled by three operators across four form types over a span of about 35 years:

| Form type | Operator | Era | Count |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hammon Field Log | Hammon Consolidated Gold Fields | ~1925 | 14 |
| Hammon Prospect Drilling Log | Hammon Consolidated (variant) | 1925 / 1936 | 5 |
| Drill Report for Frozen Ground Only | Pre-Hammon, individual operator | likely 1919 | 4 |
| Alaska Gold Company | Alaska Gold Company, Nome AK | 1955 | 1 |

The claim is roughly 600 ft × 1,000 ft. Drill depths range from 21 to 98 feet — these were churn-drill holes targeting the gold-bearing gravel layer above bedrock, the standard exploration tool for placer deposits in the period. Twenty-four holes is small enough to model and visualize directly, but the form variation (four different field-form templates over four decades) gives a representative sample of the OCR challenges a larger archive would present.

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| code-fold: true
import json
from pathlib import Path
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

REPO = Path('../..').resolve()
SD = REPO / 'data' / 'raw' / 'bear_cub' / 'structured'

intervals = pd.read_parquet(SD / 'drillhole_intervals.parquet')
water = pd.read_parquet(SD / 'drillhole_water.parquet')
yield_calcs = pd.read_parquet(SD / 'drillhole_yield_calcs.parquet')

print(f"24 logs → {len(intervals)} per-interval rows, "
      f"{len(water)} water-measurement rows, {len(yield_calcs)} back-of-sheet yield calcs")
```

## Pipeline

The pipeline turns 24 paper PDFs into a structured corpus through five stages: render PDFs to images, OCR with Claude Opus 4.7, geometric cross-check against the cartographer's property map, anchor to WGS84 via four corner GPS controls, and pattern-derive the operator's unwritten constants from the corpus. Full implementation walkthrough is in [`internal.qmd`](internal.qmd); the methodologically interesting piece is the cross-check.

### Self-correction via cross-checks

The OCR is unreliable on its own. What makes the dataset usable is that we can test it three different ways and only trust intersections:

1. **OCR confidence ratings**, reported by the model on each transcription.
2. **Geometric agreement** with a 1936 cartographer's property map: every hole has a labeled dot on the map, and a transcribed easting/northing should plot near its dot. We fit an affine transformation from local-grid feet to map pixels using 23 of 24 holes, then check whether the 24th lands within ~5 pixels of its expected position.
3. **Arithmetic closure** of the operators' yield calculations: do their cents-per-cubic-yard formulas agree with the per-interval mg + bit-area + depth they recorded?

The geometric cross-check is the strongest of the three. It caught five transcription errors in the original OCR pass that the model had reported as high-confidence:

| Hole | Original | Corrected | Error type |
|---|---|---|---|
| L2 H4 | E=74,706 | **E=76,706** | First-digit `7→4` confusion (2,000 ft west) |
| L2 H5 | E=74,706 | **E=76,713** | Same digit confusion + N tweak |
| L3 H3 | N=22,705 | N=22,702 | 3-ft northing tweak |
| L6900 H6964 | N=22,376 | **N=23,174** | 798-ft northing error |

The cross-check also rejected five later "high-confidence" re-OCR changes that would have introduced 23-340 pixel residuals had they been applied. The pipeline treats the geometric fit as the more reliable signal: when an OCR change would degrade the fit, it's reverted, regardless of the model's stated confidence.

### Pattern analysis: the 282.6 creek factor

The back of each drill log contains the operator's hand-written conversion of milligrams of recovered gold into cents per cubic yard. Different operators bundle the underlying constants — gold price, bit cross-section, unit conversions — slightly differently, and the bundled constants themselves are never written out explicitly.

To recover those constants from the corpus, we capture every back-of-page calculation as raw arithmetic and try to derive each numeric term from front-of-sheet aggregates (sum of milligrams, total depth, water-fill volumes). Terms that don't match anything derivable are clustered by value:

```{python}
#| label: constants
#| code-fold: true
counts = (yield_calcs.assign(
    terms=yield_calcs['terms_json'].apply(lambda s: json.loads(s) if s else []))
    .explode('terms')
    .pipe(lambda d: d[d.terms.notna()])
)
counts['v'] = counts['terms'].apply(lambda t: t.get('value') if isinstance(t, dict) else None)
counts['v_round'] = counts['v'].round(1)
top = counts.groupby('v_round').agg(
    count=('file_stem','size'), distinct=('file_stem','nunique')
).sort_values('count', ascending=False).head(8)
top
```

`282.6` dominates: 42 occurrences across 16 of 24 logs, spanning all three Hammon-era form types. The standard placer-prospecting formula (Forbes 1976, ch. 14) is `value (¢/cu yd) = mg × creek_factor / volume_cu_ft`; `282.6` is the specific bundling Hammon used at the period's gold price (~$20/oz) and a 5⅝-inch casing diameter. Neither the handbook nor any individual log writes the constant out explicitly — the corpus reveals it.

## Resource analysis

Two prior independent analysts have estimated the gold resource on the broader Bear Cub / Dry Gulch property using the same drill-log archive: an earlier per-interval re-transcription (2021), and a polygon-volumetric block model on the property scale (2024). They had hundreds of holes; we have a 24-hole Murray subset. We reproduce their methodology end-to-end on our subset to validate the pipeline.

### Block-volumetric estimate

The standard approach in placer-gold prospecting is a polygon block model. For each drill hole we compute a Voronoi cell — the region of the property polygon closer to that hole than to any other — and treat the cell's grade as constant and equal to the hole's grade. Multiplying cell area by depth gives a volume; multiplying volume by grade gives ounces. Sum over cells, get the property total.

The choice of *which* depth matters. Surface-to-bedrock uses the full drilled column (so the grade is depth-averaged including overburden); pay-zone-only uses just the gold-bearing layer thickness (higher grade, smaller volume). Both should give the same total ounces if all the gold is correctly attributed. Our pay-zone definition uses a sliding-window search over each hole's intervals, capping at 20 feet, to find the contiguous depth slice with the highest gold-per-foot density.

```{python}
#| label: resource-table
#| code-fold: true
res = json.loads((REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'resource_estimate.json').read_text())
print("Three resource estimates over the same 24 holes:")
print()
print(f"  Polygon, surface-to-bedrock:  {res['method_polygon']['total_fine_oz']:>6,.0f} oz at "
      f"{res['method_polygon']['weighted_avg_grade']:.4f} oz/yd³ over "
      f"{res['method_polygon']['total_volume_cuyd']:>9,.0f} cu yd")
print(f"  Triangle, surface-to-bedrock: {res['method_triangle']['total_fine_oz']:>6,.0f} oz at "
      f"{res['method_triangle']['weighted_avg_grade']:.4f} oz/yd³ over "
      f"{res['method_triangle']['total_volume_cuyd']:>9,.0f} cu yd")
print(f"  Polygon, PAY-ZONE-only:       {res['method_polygon_pay_zone']['total_fine_oz']:>6,.0f} oz at "
      f"{res['method_polygon_pay_zone']['weighted_avg_grade']:.4f} oz/yd³ over "
      f"{res['method_polygon_pay_zone']['total_volume_cuyd']:>9,.0f} cu yd  <-- comparable to published method")
print()
print(f"  Independent (2024) published:  10,056 oz at ~0.057 oz/yd³ over ~178,000 cu yd")
```

The pay-zone-only point estimate is the right number to compare against the published independent (2024) analysis. The published 10,056 oz sits outside the parameter-noise credible interval, so the gap is methodological (sliding-window pay-zone definition vs the published uniform-thickness assumption), not measurement noise.

### Uncertainty on the resource estimate

The point estimate hides several real sources of uncertainty: fineness (Bear Cub gold is ~89% fine but assays vary in the district), per-mg OCR + transcription noise (manual review precision ~5%), bit-area uncertainty (casing-vs-bit ambiguity, out-of-round drilling). A Monte Carlo wrapper propagates these:

```{python}
#| label: mc-uncertainty
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of the pay-zone-only resource estimate over 1,000 Monte Carlo samples. Fineness ∈ U(0.85, 0.92), per-mg multiplicative error ε ~ N(0, 5%), bit-area error ε ~ N(0, 5%). The 90% credible interval is narrow because errors average out across 24 holes. The published independent 10,056 oz sits well outside this interval — the gap is methodological (pay-zone definition), not measurement noise."
from IPython.display import Image
Image(str(REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'fig_mc_uncertainty.png'))
```

```{python}
#| label: mc-summary
#| code-fold: true
import json
from pathlib import Path
REPO = Path('../..').resolve()
res = json.loads((REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'resource_estimate.json').read_text())
mc = res.get('uncertainty_pay_zone_oz', {})
if mc.get('p05') is not None:
    print(f"Pay-zone-only resource — 90% credible interval over {mc['n_samples']} MC samples:")
    print(f"  5%:    {mc['p05']:>5,.0f} fine oz")
    print(f"  50%:   {mc['p50']:>5,.0f} fine oz")
    print(f"  95%:   {mc['p95']:>5,.0f} fine oz")
    print(f"  mean:  {mc['mean']:>5,.0f} fine oz")
```

The 90% credible interval is narrow because per-hole errors average out across 24 holes. What this Monte Carlo *doesn't* capture is **structural** uncertainty: the choice of pay-zone window cap (12 vs 20 vs 30 ft), the bedrock-imputation approach for NBR holes, the color-weighted distribution heuristic for Convention C samples. Those are methodology decisions; their effect would be quantified by running multiple methodology variants and comparing — separate from this parameter-noise interval.

### Cross-validation: shared holes

Three of our holes have published per-hole grades from the two prior independent analyses (2021 and 2024). The comparison surfaces the key methodological lever — *how each calculation defines the pay zone* — much more clearly than the property-wide totals do:

```{python}
#| label: cross-val
#| code-fold: true
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path
REPO = Path('../..').resolve()
cv = pd.read_csv(REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'cross_validation.csv')
cv
```

`L6700 H6760` is the only hole with a 2024-published number to compare against: our sliding-window picks a tight 0.094 oz/cu yd over a 2 ft (40-42 ft) bedrock-contact band; the published 0.050 is over an 18 ft (30-48 ft) wider window. The total recovered gold over the published window is consistent with our depth-weighted reading on the same window — the difference is purely the assumed thickness over which to average. The two L7100 Convention C holes show the same pattern against the 2021 analyst's tighter pay-zone picks.

The mining-engineer review notebook at [`mining_review.qmd`](mining_review.html) walks through the methodology in the depth a placer engineer would want.

## Visualizations

### Aerial overlay

Drill holes and Voronoi pay-zone cells overlaid on a high-resolution satellite image (ESRI World Imagery, ~1 m/pixel). This places Bear Cub in its actual physical setting near Nome — the post-2021 Dry Gulch settling ponds, the second mining cut crossing the property, and the surrounding tundra and stream channels are all visible.

```{python}
#| label: aerial-overlay
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Bear Cub MS 1178 on satellite imagery. Drill holes color-coded by pay-zone avg grade."
from IPython.display import Image
Image(str(REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'fig_aerial_overlay.png'))
```

```{python}
#| label: aerial-pay-zone
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Same view with the pay-zone Voronoi cells filled in (polygon-block grade map adjusted to bottom-of-pay)."
Image(str(REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'fig_aerial_pay_zone_grade.png'))
```

### Pay-zone grade map (no aerial)

```{python}
#| label: pay-zone-map
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Voronoi cells colored by pay-zone average grade, on the local-feet grid. The southern + south-central holes show the highest concentrations."
Image(str(REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'fig_pay_zone_grade_map.png'))
```

### Year of drilling

```{python}
#| label: year-map
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Holes color-coded by drilling year. 1919 Frozen Ground forms (NW corner), 1925 + 1936 Hammon work (most of the dataset), and a single 1955 Alaska Gold hole."
Image(str(REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'fig_year_map.png'))
```

### 3D drill-hole model

```{python}
#| label: model-3d
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "PyVista 3D scene: 24 drill collars as vertical sticks segmented at every captured 2 ft interval, colored by per-interval grade. Bedrock topography is a thin-plate spline interpolation. Vertical exaggeration 8× because the claim is ~1100 ft long but only ~80 ft deep. The bedrock-contact pay signature reads at the bottom of the marked sticks. Interactive WebGL version: [bear_cub_3d.html](../../data/derived/bear_cub_resource/bear_cub_3d.html)."
Image(str(REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'fig_3d_profile_east.png'))
```

## What this doesn't prove

This is twenty-four holes on one claim, drilled by closely related operators using closely related field forms. The pipeline is built and the cross-checking methodology is the deliverable, but generalizing to a 100-log or 1,000-log archive across multiple properties, operators, and eras is the obvious next step — and not the same problem at scale.

The validation is geometric (do the holes project cleanly onto the cartographer's map?) and arithmetic (do the operator's yield formulas close from front-of-sheet inputs?), not predictive accuracy on held-out data. This isn't a predictive model.

OCR cost roughly $14 in this run, or about $0.50-$1.00 per multi-page log. A 10,000-log archive at the same per-log cost would be substantial. The hybrid architecture (Document AI for raw text, smaller LLM pass for schema mapping) should drop the per-log cost by an order of magnitude, but I haven't demonstrated that yet on this archive.

## Spatial predictive model + drill-site recommender

Once the corpus is structured, the obvious next thing is to fit a model to it. A Gaussian-process regressor over hole positions takes (x, y) → predicted grade with a per-location uncertainty estimate; the same model output drives a drill-site recommender that picks the next hole to maximize expected information gain or expected improvement against a target grade.

**Two targets, side-by-side:**

```{python}
#| label: gp-loo
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Leave-one-out cross-validation of a spatial-only Gaussian-process regressor (anisotropic RBF + white-noise kernel). Left: pay-zone average grade (sharply spiky from beach-line geology — n=24 isn't enough to learn the gradient). Right: surface-to-bedrock grade (smoother, depth-averaged). Error bars = predictive σ."
from IPython.display import Image
Image(str(REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'fig_gp_loo_residuals.png'))
```

```{python}
#| label: gp-metrics
#| code-fold: true
import json
from pathlib import Path
REPO = Path('../..').resolve()
gp = json.loads((REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'predictive_model_metrics.json').read_text())
print("LOO-CV results:")
print(f"  Pay-zone avg grade:  MAE {gp['loo_cv_pay_zone_avg_grade']['mae']:.4f}  RMSE {gp['loo_cv_pay_zone_avg_grade']['rmse']:.4f}  R² {gp['loo_cv_pay_zone_avg_grade']['r2']:.3f}")
print(f"  Surface-to-BR grade: MAE {gp['loo_cv_surface_to_br_grade']['mae']:.4f}  RMSE {gp['loo_cv_surface_to_br_grade']['rmse']:.4f}  R² {gp['loo_cv_surface_to_br_grade']['r2']:.3f}")
```

**Honest finding: a spatial-only GP doesn't predict either target well on n=24.** Negative R² means the model performs worse than just predicting the dataset mean. The reason is geological: pay-zone grade has a sharp gradient at the beach line (a line crossing the property where placer gold concentrates), and 24 spatial samples can't constrain a gradient that steep. With more holes, or with auxiliary geological features (a "beach-line distance" indicator, formation type, hole depth), the model would have something to fit.

**The drill-site recommender output is still meaningful, even with a weak predictive model.** The recommender selects locations that maximize either (a) GP predictive variance — areas the model is most uncertain about, where a new hole would yield the most information — or (b) expected improvement against a 0.05 oz/yd³ target — areas with the best combination of high predicted grade and high uncertainty.

```{python}
#| label: gp-recommender
#| code-fold: true
#| fig-cap: "Recommended next-drill locations on the GP-predicted grade surface. Cyan triangles maximize predictive variance (info gain). Magenta stars maximize expected improvement against a 0.05 oz/yd³ target. Both criteria exclude points within 80 ft of an existing hole."
Image(str(REPO / 'data' / 'derived' / 'bear_cub_resource' / 'fig_gp_drill_recommender.png'))
```

The two criteria recommend different locations: maximum-variance picks the gaps in the existing drill grid (largest empty spaces between holes), while expected-improvement picks gaps that are also near already-known high-grade holes — leveraging spatial correlation under the GP. For a real exploration program with a budget for one more hole, the EI criterion is what most planners would use; for a research campaign focused on understanding the deposit, max-variance is more informative.

This is a small piece of what published POMDP drill planning methods (Mern et al. *Intelligent Prospector* v1.0/v2.0, the Caers Mineral-X line at Stanford) do at full scale — multi-step decision making under uncertainty, with drilling cost vs information value tradeoffs. The Bear Cub data is far too small for a real planner — but the methodology is the same shape.

## Where this could go

A few natural extensions, each of which could stand on its own:

1. **A surveyor's toolkit** — given a few field GPS readings plus a patent description, anchor the local survey grid in WGS84. The four-corner affine and BLM-patent-walker code I built for Bear Cub is the seed.
2. **A general drill-log CLI** — `drill-log → structured CSV → 3D model → yield estimate` as a standalone tool, lifted out of this specific archive.
3. **Broader dark-data ingestion** — the same approach applied to the rest of the records associated with a mining property: court filings, surveyors' notes, title histories, family correspondence. Pulling that material into a single linked dataset with full provenance is the broader version of the dark-data ingestion pattern that AI-exploration companies are publicly working on.

## References

- Forbes, M., 1976. *Handbook for the Alaskan Prospector*, ch. 14: "Drilling Placer Deposits". Methodology and worked yield-calculation examples cited throughout. Saved at [`data/raw/bear_cub/reference/Handbook_for_the_Alaskan_Prospector.pdf`](../../data/raw/bear_cub/reference/Handbook_for_the_Alaskan_Prospector.pdf).
- BLM, 2014. McClintock Re-Survey of MS 1178 (Bear Cub Placer Claim, Cape Nome mining district), Scott McClintock RLS 8904-3. Source for the four-corner WGS84 anchor.
- Reed, I.McK., 1908. Patent plat for MS 1178. Course descriptions used for the four-corner reconstruction.

Source: family-held drill-log archive, Murray group. Methodology and schema-only CSV publishable; raw scans private.
